schema.name: pm.core.project
schema.description: Core project governance model for the Pytagoras Fullstack Python/Next.js Template.

project.name: Pytagoras Fullstack Python/Next.js Template
project.description: A production-ready, feature-rich fullstack starter template combining Next.js (App Router, Bun, shadcn/ui, Clerk, next-intl, Zustand) frontend with FastAPI (SQLAlchemy 2, Alembic, Pydantic v2, uv) backend. Vertical slice architecture, dependency injection, OpenRouter AI, Railway deployment.

objectives.name: Production-ready fullstack template
objectives.description: Deliver a complete, working starter template that developers can clone and build upon immediately.
successCriteria: docker-compose up starts both services successfully
Authenticated chat with conversation history works
Admin panel accessible only to admin users
All tests pass (bun test and pytest)
All linters pass (biome check and ruff check)

objectives.name: Reusable foundation for HQ and LMS
objectives.description: The template becomes the starter scaffold for all future Pytagoras web projects, including the HQ mission control center and the LMS.
successCriteria: Template is usable as a GitHub template repository
README enables a new developer to be running in under 5 minutes

inScope: Next.js frontend with App Router, Bun, shadcn/ui, Tailwind
FastAPI backend with SQLAlchemy 2, Alembic, Pydantic v2
Four vertical slices: Landing (guest chat), Dashboard (auth chat), Profile, Admin
Backend tests (pytest) and frontend tests (bun test)

constraints.time: Template must be usable as foundation for HQ before HQ development begins.

cost.note: Cost is API usage (Claude Code headless sessions). No hard cap.

quality: All linters must pass before template is published
docker-compose up must work out of the box

assumptions: Claude Code is installed and authenticated on the development machine.
Clerk account and API keys are available.
Neon PostgreSQL database is provisioned with production and staging branches.
Railway CLI and Neon CLI are installed and authenticated on the development machine.
GitHub branch workflow is dev -> staging -> main, with phase work performed on phase-X branches created from dev.
Railway runtime uses pooled Neon DATABASE_URL values, while direct Neon connection strings are fetched only for migration or admin tasks when needed.

dependencies.description: Clerk API keys required for auth setup.
OpenRouter API key required for AI integration.
Neon PostgreSQL DATABASE_URL required for backend.
Railway project must contain separate frontend and backend services linked to the GitHub repository before environment sync automation can populate production and staging variables.
Railway staging environment and public domains must exist so cross-service Railway reference variables resolve to production and staging URLs correctly.

statusSummary.summary: Phase 7 is underway. Docker, docker-compose, CI, README/PRD updates, Railway health alignment, and the Railway/Neon env sync script are implemented locally. Railway service splitting and live GitHub-linked staging/production validation remain open.
topIssues: Railway project still needs separate frontend and backend services wired to the GitHub monorepo branch workflow
Production and staging Railway environments still need live service/domain validation after repo-side Phase 7 artifacts were added
Phase 6 frontend/backend integration still needs full end-to-end credential-backed validation alongside the deployment work

execution.description: Operational context sent to Claude Code as the system prompt before every activity. Covers how to start the stack, run checks, verify in browser, log progress, and commit. The activity steps and criteria are appended after this as the task prompt.

stack.note: Always use 127.0.0.1 instead of localhost on Windows. Always check if services are already running before starting them. Always stop services after completing a task.

frontend: docker compose exec frontend bun run lint
docker compose exec frontend bun run build
backend: docker compose exec backend uv run ruff check .
docker compose exec backend uv run mypy .

steps: Open the local server URL: agent-browser open <url>
Wait for hydration: agent-browser wait --load networkidle

progressLog.instruction: Append a dated entry describing: what changed, commands run, screenshot filename, issues encountered and how resolved.

commit.instruction: One git commit per activity. Message format: 'feat: <brief description>'. Stage all changed files. Do NOT run git init, change remotes, or push.
commit.template: feat: <brief description of what was implemented>

rules: Work from a phase-X branch created from dev when executing a planned phase.
Use docker compose for local development validation and GitHub-linked Railway services for staging/production deployment work.
Use uv run scripts/sync_railway_env.py to provision Railway variables from local env files and Neon branch connection strings when Phase 7 deployment automation is in scope.
Always run the relevant lint/type/build checks before marking an activity complete.
Always log progress in activity.md and update the activity criteria status in .project/schedule.json when done.
Always stop any services you started after the activity is complete.
execution.systemPrompt:
You are an autonomous developer working on the Pytagoras Fullstack Python/Next.js Template. Your job is to complete the assigned implementation work while keeping project metadata, deployment automation, and documentation aligned with repo truth.

Environment notes for this Windows machine:
- uv is available on PATH for standalone automation scripts such as uv run scripts/sync_railway_env.py.
- For app validation, prefer docker compose exec frontend bun run <script> and docker compose exec backend uv run <command>.
- Always use 127.0.0.1 instead of localhost for local browser-facing URLs on Windows where practical.
- Do not create files under frontend/src/app/.cache or any .cache directory.

Before starting an activity:
1. Run git log --oneline -10 to understand recent repo state.
2. Read activity.md for the recent execution log and known issues.
3. Check docker compose ps and start the stack only if the planned validation needs it and it is not already running.

During execution:
1. Implement the required changes in the current working branch.
2. Run the relevant checks for the touched subsystems and fix failures before continuing.
3. Update activity.md with what changed, commands run, and notable issues.
4. Update .project/schedule.json when an activity is complete or materially advanced.
5. Keep README and deployment docs aligned with the actual workflow when infrastructure behavior changes.

Do not push to git. When Railway deployment work is in scope, treat docker-compose.yml as local-only and use GitHub-linked Railway services plus the environment sync script for deployed environments.
